title: Leveraging Dimensions for Research Reporting
description:
Please join us for a training session tailored to UCSF's reporting needs. This session will be led by Caroline Crossman, Dimensions Engagement Manager, who will walk you through how to get the most out of Dimensions to support reports on research activity and impact. Topics will include:

  • Reporting at the university level, using the Research Organization and additional filters to refine search results

  • Reporting on research departments, institutes, and labs by creating custom Groups in Dimensions or by using the ID Search to explore a subset of publications specific to your group

  • Exploring researcher profiles in Dimensions to assess research productivity and showcase research impact using citations and altmetrics 

Please note that this session will be recorded.

About Dimensions 

Dimensions is a comprehensive research intelligence platform that connects publications, grants, clinical trials, patents, and policy documents in one place. With powerful search and analytics capabilities, it enables institutions, research groups, and individual researchers to track funding landscapes, measure research impact through citation and Altmetric data, and map collaborations.

Find out more about UCSF's access to Dimensions, and access the application with your UCSF email address at app.dimensions.ai
featured_image_alt_text: screen capture of the Dimensions logo, with "A Digital Science Solution" below it

title: AI Summer Discussion Club: Are we heading for AI dystopia?
description:
Thinking about artificial intelligence this summer? Join our AI Summer Discussion Club in person at the FAMRI Library in Mission Bay! Each month, we will discuss 2-3 articles, videos, or news pieces about different AI topics.

We will have tea and light snacks. Feel free to sign up for single sessions or all of them!

 

The reading/watching commitment for this series is relatively light (15-30 min of content), and discussion questions will be shared with registrants before the session. Reading/watching assigned content in advance and taking some notes is recommended, but not required for participation.

Readings

Goudarzi, S. (2026, May 25). AI can chart a course to disaster faster than humans can notice. Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists. https://thebulletin.org/2026/05/ai-can-chart-a-course-to-disaster-faster-than-humans-can-notice/

An AI disaster is getting ever closer. (2026, March 5). The Economist. Retrieved July 2, 2026, from https://www.economist.com/briefing/2026/03/05/an-ai-disaster-is-getting-ever-closer

  • For those without subscription access to The Economist, San Francisco Public Library provides access through Gale if you have a library card: https://link.gale.com/apps/doc/A877507989/AONE?u=sfpl_main&sid=bookmark-AONE&xid=d8278930

 

Optional:

Germain, T. (2026, April 29). Why AI companies want you to be afraid of them. BBC. https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20260428-ai-companies-want-you-to-be-afraid-of-them

 

Accessibility

UCSF welcomes all participants to our events. If you need a reasonable accommodation to participate in this event because of a disability, please contact Eileen Chen at eileen.chen@ucsf.edu as soon as possible.

 

People

  •  
  • Eileen Chen is an Education and Research Librarian. She supports literature searching, AI, and open access publishing.
  • Geoff Boushey is a Data Science Specialist. He teaches with Python, SQL, machine learning, and AI, with an emphasis on document, text, and media analysis.
  • Yea-Hung Chen is a Data Science Specialist. He provides support on R, data science, and AI to the UCSF community.

title: Needlecraft Monthly Meet-Up - August
description:
Calling all fans of knitting, crochet, cross-stitch, sewing, and embroidery!

This virtual meet-up is a chance for beginning and experienced UCSF crafters to come together and work on their projects in a fun and relaxed atmosphere. We hang, we chat, we work on fabulous projects, and share advice and inspiration. Join us!

Register below to receive the Zoom link and password. See all upcoming Needlecraft Meet-Ups to save your seat today!

All Makers Lab pop-ups are open to the UCSF community. If you are a DPH employee, UCSF alum or retiree, please contact the Makers Lab to register for this pop-up.

Photo from UCSF staff member Ariel

Have questions about the Makers Lab? Visit the Makers Lab webpage or contact us. The Makers Lab is open by appointment for UCSF teaching, learning, and research projects. Contact the Makers Lab to schedule an appointment.

title: R: How can I do math?
description:
About the session

This session will cover: 

  • RStudio's panes
  • the RStudio console
  • mathematical operators
  • mathematical functions
  • object assignment
  • quitting

This is the first session in the A Slower Introduction to R workshop series.

Registration and reminders

Please register for the course by clicking the registration button at the bottom of the page. We suggest adding the event, with the Zoom link, to your calendar when you receive your confirmation email. ☺️ 

About the series

A Slower Introduction to R is a 7-session workshop series on R, focusing on common data-related tasks. Each session will involve short presentations interspersed with hands-on exercises.

We do not assume any prior experience with R or programming. 

The website for the workshop series, which includes slides and other course material, is here.

About the instructor

The instructor, Yea-Hung Chen, PhD, MS, has more than 10 years of experience teaching R to the UCSF community. He designed and taught UCSF's first-ever R course (Biostat 213) and taught the introductory biostatistics course for UCSF's MS in Global Health program (GHS 207). 

Accessibility statement

UCSF welcomes all participants to our events. If you need a reasonable accommodation to participate in this event because of a disability, please contact Yea-Hung Chen at Yea-hung.Chen@ucsf.edu as soon as possible.

title: Bay Area Open Science Group - Evolving approaches to open science and research transparency education
description:
This month we are joined by Jo Weech, Research Transparency Program Manager at the Berkeley Initiative for Transparency in the Social Sciences BITSS. Jo supports teams working on academic forecasting, evidence aggregation, and reporting in economics. In addition to its research projects, BITSS hosts an annual conference and course on research transparency. Her presentation for the September meeting will be on how education in open science and research transparency has evolved due to changes in researcher norms and use of AI.
 
The Bay Area Open Science Group is a growing community for Bay Area academics and researchers interested in incorporating open science into their research, teaching, and learning. Targeting students, faculty, and staff at UCSF, Berkeley, and Stanford, the goal of the community is to increase awareness of and engagement with all things open science, including open access articles, open research data, open source software, and open educational resources. Through this work the group hopes to connect researchers with tools they can use to make the products and process of science more equitable and reproducible.
 

UCSF welcomes all participants to our events. If you need a reasonable accommodation to participate in this event because of a disability, please contact Ariel Deardorff at ariel.deardorff@ucsf.edu as soon as possible.

Register for this event via Zoom

title: R: How can I import data?
description:
About the session

This session will cover: 

  • scripts
  • suggested workflow
  • importing data
  • dollar-sign notation
  • object classes

This is the second session in the A Slower Introduction to R workshop series.

Registration and reminders

Please register for the course by clicking the registration button at the bottom of the page. We suggest adding the event, with the Zoom link, to your calendar when you receive your confirmation email. ☺️
